channel.title: Cobalt Shields - What We're Reading Today
channel.description: Cobalt Shields — Trusted | Reliable. Daily IT, cybersecurity, and infrastructure reading list curated for healthcare, higher ed, government, K-12, and nonprofit leaders.

image.title: Cobalt Shields - What We're Reading Today

item.title: Microsoft patches SharePoint RCE flaw CVE-2026-45659 across on-premises Server versions
item.description: Microsoft released a fix for CVE-2026-45659 (CVSS 8.8), a deserialization-of-untrusted-data flaw in on-premises SharePoint Server that lets an authenticated user with only Site Member permissions execute code remotely — no administrator rights required. The bug affects SharePoint Server Subscription Edition, SharePoint Server 2019, and SharePoint Enterprise Server 2016. Microsoft rates it "less likely to be exploited" and no in-the-wild attacks have been reported, but SharePoint flaws have been repeatedly weaponized, so administrators of self-hosted farms should patch promptly. On-prem SharePoint remains common across higher-ed, healthcare, and government, making this a priority update for those IT teams. | Source: The Hacker News

item.title: Iranian group MuddyWater uses signed-binary DLL side-loading to hit education and public-sector targets across nine countries
item.description: Researchers detailed a refined MuddyWater (Seedworm) espionage campaign in which the IRGC-linked Iranian group abused legitimately signed Fortemedia and SentinelOne binaries to side-load malicious DLLs, then ran Node.js-launched PowerShell for reconnaissance and credential theft via the ChromElevator tool. Nine organizations across four continents were hit — spanning education, public sector, financial services, industrial manufacturing, and professional services — with attackers dwelling about a week inside one South Korean victim and staging stolen data on the public sendit.sh service. The operators showed markedly improved operational security over prior campaigns. Education and government IT teams should hunt for anomalous use of signed vendor binaries and unexpected Node.js or PowerShell activity. Companion analysis: https://www.securityweek.com/iranian-apt-targets-aviation-software-companies-with-updated-tools/ | Source: The Hacker News

item.title: Microsoft Defender for Endpoint can now automatically isolate compromised devices
item.description: Microsoft is previewing an automatic device-isolation capability in Defender for Endpoint that, as part of "automatic attack disruption," cuts a suspected-compromised workstation off from the network while keeping it connected to the Defender service for continued monitoring. The feature aims to contain threats and limit lateral movement without waiting for analyst action, and security teams can manually release devices after investigating through the device inventory. It currently works only on onboarded end-user workstations and remains in preview. The change is relevant to resource-constrained healthcare, education, and government SOCs weighing automated containment against the risk of disrupting legitimate endpoints. | Source: BleepingComputer

item.title: Hard-coded ASP.NET keys in KnowledgeDeliver LMS (CVE-2026-5426) exploited to deploy Godzilla web shell and Cobalt Strike at schools
item.description: Google Mandiant disclosed that attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-5426 (CVSS 7.5), a hard-coded ASP.NET machine-key flaw in Digital Knowledge's KnowledgeDeliver learning management system that enables unauthenticated remote code execution through ViewState deserialization. After gaining access, the operators drop the Godzilla web shell and tamper with site JavaScript to show fake "security plugin" prompts that ultimately deliver a Cobalt Strike Beacon to visitors. KnowledgeDeliver is widely deployed across educational institutions, making this a direct threat to K-12 and higher-ed IT teams. Administrators should upgrade to the patched release (post–February 24, 2026), replace any shared machine keys with unique secrets, and deploy endpoint monitoring. | Source: The Hacker News

item.title: Lithuania suspects foreign actor behind leak of 600,000+ national register records
item.description: Lithuania's State Enterprise Centre of Registers disclosed that more than 600,000 entries from the country's real-estate and legal-entity registers were accessed using stolen credentials belonging to institutions authorized to query the data. Officials suspect a foreign state — an opposition politician pointed to Russia without providing evidence — amid Lithuania's standing as a frequent target of hybrid operations against Europe. Authorities have blocked the suspected accounts and now require credential updates for register access, and the agency's head resigned over the incident. The breach is a reminder for government data custodians that authorized third-party credentials are a prime avenue for large-scale register compromise. | Source: SecurityWeek

item.title: Anthropic's restricted Claude Mythos model spotted heading to Claude Code
item.description: BleepingComputer reports that Anthropic appears to be preparing a public rollout of Claude Mythos, the frontier model announced in April 2026 as a restricted system because it can autonomously develop professional-grade cyberattacks. Users briefly spotted a "claude-mythos-1-preview" toggle in Claude Code before it was removed, and the model has also surfaced in Claude Security. Mythos is the engine behind Anthropic's Glasswing program, which surfaced more than 10,000 high- and critical-severity vulnerabilities in its first month. IT leaders evaluating AI-assisted development should weigh both the defensive upside and the dual-use risk as these capabilities reach mainstream tooling. | Source: BleepingComputer

item.title: FBI warns of Kali365 phishing-as-a-service abusing OAuth device codes to hijack Microsoft 365 accounts
item.description: The FBI issued an alert about Kali365, a phishing-as-a-service platform that emerged in April 2026 and is sold via Telegram to compromise Microsoft 365 and Entra accounts without stealing passwords or intercepting MFA codes. It abuses Microsoft's legitimate OAuth 2.0 device-authorization flow — tricking victims into entering an attacker-generated device code and completing MFA — to hand the attacker a full-access token, and offers an adversary-in-the-middle "Cookie Link" mode to capture authenticated sessions. Attackers have accessed mailboxes and created hidden inbox rules to mask activity; similar kits like EvilTokens and Tycoon2FA use the same technique. The FBI urges organizations to restrict or block device-code authentication via Conditional Access, audit existing device-code usage, and report incidents to IC3. | Source: BleepingComputer

item.title: TrapDoor cross-ecosystem supply chain attack hits npm, PyPI, and Crates.io and poisons AI coding assistants
item.description: Socket researchers disclosed TrapDoor, a coordinated supply chain campaign that planted more than 34 malicious packages across 384+ versions on npm, PyPI, and Crates.io to steal SSH keys, AWS credentials, GitHub tokens, browser data, and crypto wallets from developers. Most notably, the operators poisoned AI coding assistants by embedding zero-width-Unicode instructions in CLAUDE.md and .cursorrules files submitted via pull requests to popular projects including LangChain, LlamaIndex, and MetaGPT, tricking tools like Cursor and Claude Code into running a fake "security scan" that exfiltrates secrets. The technique extends supply chain risk from package installs to the AI agents now embedded in many development workflows. Healthcare, higher-ed, and government teams adopting AI-assisted coding should pin dependencies, review agent configuration files, and rotate exposed credentials. Companion analysis: https://socket.dev/blog/trapdoor-crypto-stealer-npm-pypi-crates | Source: The Hacker News

item.title: Actively exploited Ghost CMS SQL injection (CVE-2026-26980) fuels large-scale ClickFix campaign hitting university portals
item.description: Attackers are mass-exploiting CVE-2026-26980, an unauthenticated SQL injection flaw (CVSS 9.4) in the Ghost CMS Content API affecting versions 3.24.0 through 6.19.0 that exposes admin API keys. XLab researchers found the campaign has compromised more than 700 domains — including university portals, media outlets, and SaaS and fintech firms — by stealing API keys and bulk-injecting malicious JavaScript that serves fake Cloudflare "ClickFix" prompts tricking visitors into pasting attacker commands into Windows. Administrators running Ghost should upgrade immediately, rotate all Ghost Admin and Content API keys and admin credentials, and audit posts for injected loader scripts. Companion analysis: https://blog.xlab.qianxin.com/ghost-cms-mass-compromised-via-cve-2026-26980-now-fueling-clickfix-attacks/ | Source: BleepingComputer

item.title: Security Affairs weekly roundup: Drupal KEV exploitation, Laravel-Lang and Megalodon supply chain attacks, and AI bug-hunting fallout
item.description: Security Affairs' May 24 international newsletter rounds up the week's top threats, including active exploitation of the Drupal Core SQL injection (CVE-2026-9082) now in CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, the Laravel-Lang and Megalodon software supply chain campaigns, and the patch-management strain created by AI-assisted vulnerability discovery. It is a useful weekly scan for healthcare, higher-ed, government, and nonprofit IT teams tracking the fast-moving supply chain and exploitation landscape. Companion malware digest: https://securityaffairs.com/192598/malware/security-affairs-malware-newsletter-round-98.html | Source: Security Affairs

item.title: Charter Communications confirms breach as ShinyHunters threatens to leak 42 million records
item.description: Charter Communications, the U.S. cable and broadband provider behind the Spectrum brand, confirmed it is investigating a cybersecurity incident after the ShinyHunters extortion group listed it on a leak site claiming more than 42 million records of personally identifiable information. The group issued a "final warning" dated May 23 demanding negotiations begin by May 27 or it will release the data. Charter said no sensitive personal information or customer proprietary network information (CPNI) was exfiltrated and that it is cooperating with authorities. The listing extends ShinyHunters' 2026 campaign against Salesforce and enterprise cloud environments that also hit Instructure/Canvas. | Source: CyberInsider

item.title: CISA adds actively exploited Drupal Core SQL injection (CVE-2026-9082) to KEV catalog
item.description: CISA added CVE-2026-9082, a critical SQL injection flaw in Drupal Core's database abstraction API affecting PostgreSQL-backed sites, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirming active exploitation in the wild. The unauthenticated bug affects Drupal 8.0 through 11.3.9 and can enable information disclosure, data modification, and in some configurations remote code execution. Imperva reported more than 15,000 exploitation attempts against roughly 6,000 sites across 65 countries within two days of disclosure, and federal agencies face a mandatory patch deadline. Higher-ed, government, and nonprofit operators running Drupal on PostgreSQL should update immediately. Companion analysis: https://www.tenable.com/blog/cve-2026-9082-highly-critical-sql-injection-vulnerability-in-drupal-core-sa-core-2026-004 | Source: Security Affairs

item.title: Supply Chain Attacks, AI Security, and Major Breaches Define Cybersecurity This Week in May 2026
item.description: eSecurity Planet's weekly roundup covers the Laravel-Lang supply chain attack, the Megalodon GitHub commit-injection campaign, ongoing Drupal exploitation, and the broader trend of AI-assisted offensive security tooling lowering the bar for sophisticated attacks against enterprise targets. | Source: eSecurity Planet

item.title: Anthropic's Project Glasswing reports 10,000+ critical software vulnerabilities found in one month
item.description: Anthropic said partners using its Claude Mythos Preview model under Project Glasswing collectively identified more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities across systemically important software in roughly a month, including over 6,200 in more than 1,000 open-source projects. Several partners reported bug-discovery gains exceeding tenfold; Cloudflare alone found about 2,000 bugs, 400 of them high or critical. The bottleneck has shifted from finding flaws to verifying, disclosing, and patching them, with some open-source maintainers asking Anthropic to slow disclosures. The trend reshapes vulnerability-management and patch-prioritization planning for healthcare, higher-ed, and government IT teams. | Source: Anthropic

item.title: Software Supply Chain Attack Targets Multiple PHP Laravel-Lang Packages
item.description: Cybersecurity researchers disclosed a fresh software supply chain attack campaign targeting multiple PHP packages belonging to Laravel-Lang. Affected packages and tags were published in rapid succession on May 22–23, 2026. Developers are advised to audit composer.lock files immediately. | Source: The Hacker News

item.title: Trump cancels AI executive order signing as California's Newsom signs worker-protection AI order
item.description: President Trump scrapped a planned Oval Office signing of a new federal AI order on May 22, telling reporters he did not like the text and worried regulation could blunt the U.S. lead over China. Hours later, California Governor Gavin Newsom signed a state executive order directing the labor department to study AI's impact on workers, expand unemployment insurance enrollment, and partner with UC and Stanford on public-interest AI. The split underscores a widening federal-state divide on AI governance that affects how higher-ed, healthcare, and government IT leaders plan procurement and workforce policy. Companion NPR coverage: https://www.npr.org/2026/05/22/g-s1-123671/ca-gov-gavin-newsom-signed-an-executive-order-to-protect-workers-from-ai | Source: NPR

item.title: DHS says ICE has 'no relationship' with spyware maker Paragon Solutions
item.description: The Department of Homeland Security told NPR on May 22 that Immigration and Customs Enforcement has no current contract or relationship with Israeli-founded spyware vendor Paragon Solutions, and that a previously revived contract was closed out on January 20. Advocates noted DHS declined to clarify whether ICE retains access to Paragon-developed tools through a third party, leaving open compliance questions under the 2023 executive order restricting commercial spyware. The episode highlights ongoing federal procurement, audit, and civil-liberties scrutiny around government use of mobile-device exploitation tools. | Source: NPR

item.title: Instructure strikes deal with ShinyHunters over Canvas hack; NWT government says ~1,700 employees and contractors affected
item.description: Instructure, parent of the Canvas learning management system, confirmed it reached an agreement with the ShinyHunters group claiming responsibility for the April–May breach affecting roughly 9,000 institutions worldwide. The company said it received assurance that no customers would be extorted as a result, but did not disclose whether payment was involved. Separately, the Government of the Northwest Territories disclosed that contact and training records for about 1,700 teachers, government employees, and contractors may have been exposed. Higher-ed, K-12, and public-sector IT teams that use Canvas should review contractual notification obligations and incident-response playbooks. NWT coverage: https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/north/canvas-breach-nwt-9.7208039 | Source: CBC News

item.title: Ottawa man charged in Canada and U.S. over Kimwolf botnet operation that infected millions of devices
item.description: Ontario Provincial Police arrested 23-year-old Jacob Butler of Ottawa, who is accused of helping develop and operate the Kimwolf botnet that, with Aisuru, infected millions of IoT devices including IPs on the U.S. Department of Defense Information Network. U.S. prosecutors say they tied Butler to the operation through IP addresses, online accounts, transaction records, and messaging logs obtained via legal process. The case follows the earlier disruption of the Kimwolf DDoS-for-hire service and underscores increasing law-enforcement focus on IoT-based botnets that disrupt government, healthcare, and education networks. | Source: CBC News

item.title: Megalodon Campaign Pushes 5,718 Malicious Commits to GitHub Repositories in Six Hours
item.description: A new automated campaign called Megalodon pushed 5,718 malicious commits to 5,561 GitHub repositories within a six-hour window. The campaign targets CI/CD pipelines by injecting malicious workflow steps. GitHub has suspended the associated accounts. | Source: SecurityWeek

item.title: Saskatchewan nonprofit TeleMiracle / Kinsmen Foundation warns donors of possible data leak
item.description: The Kinsmen Foundation, which runs Saskatchewan's annual TeleMiracle telethon, told donors on May 22 that names, email addresses, phone numbers, residential addresses, and donation amounts may have been exposed in a breach of its systems. Saskatoon police are investigating and the foundation says systems have been secured with additional safeguards added. The incident is a reminder that even small-to-mid-size charities hold rich PII and require donor-CRM hardening and breach-notification playbooks. | Source: CBC News

item.title: U.S. tech lobby urges Ottawa to withdraw lawful access bill, citing encryption and trade risks
item.description: The Information Technology Industry Council — whose members include Amazon, Google, and Nvidia — formally called on the Canadian government to pull Bill C-22, arguing the lawful access regime has extraterritorial reach and creates conflict-of-law issues for global providers. Apple, Meta, Signal, and several VPN providers have publicly warned the bill threatens encryption guarantees; CSIS and the RCMP continue to lobby for passage by summer. The dispute is shaping cross-border tech procurement, encryption policy, and compliance planning for any Canadian healthcare, higher-ed, or government entity that relies on U.S. cloud or messaging providers. | Source: CBC News

item.title: Lawmakers warn U.S. data-broker location-protection rules leave White House, Congress, and CIA exposed
item.description: Senators Ron Wyden and Martin Heinrich and Representative Sara Jacobs warned the Trump administration that the April 2025 rules limiting commercial location-data sales to U.S. adversaries omit 736 sensitive locations including the White House, Congress, and CIA headquarters. The lawmakers say data brokers' sale of Americans' location data poses a serious national-security risk when government-employee movements can be mapped by foreign services. They urged a "protection zone" covering all of Washington, D.C. — a development federal contractors and any organization with sensitive personnel should track for compliance impact. | Source: PBS NewsHour

item.title: New Infosec Products of the Week: May 22, 2026
item.description: Help Net Security's weekly roundup includes Trust3 AI's MCP Security standard for safeguarding enterprise agentic AI, Babel Street's Insights Investigator for AI-assisted investigations, and several new SIEM and threat-intelligence integrations announced at RSA Conference 2026. | Source: Help Net Security
